docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Modeling, AI, and Optimization for Engineering Design

We develop methods for simulation-based design optimization, dimensionality reduction, surrogate modeling, and machine learning for complex engineering systems, with applications in marine, aerospace, and multidisciplinary vehicle design.

Research People Projects Software

docs.title: About
docs.text:
The MAO Research Group works on advanced computational methods for engineering design, with a focus on:

  • simulation-based design optimization
  • dimensionality reduction for shape optimization
  • physics-informed and multi-fidelity surrogate models
  • machine learning for marine and aerospace applications
  • reproducible computational workflows and open scientific software

docs.title: Research Areas
docs.text:
Dimensionality Reduction

Reduced-order representations of high-dimensional design spaces, including PME and its physics-informed variants.

Simulation-Based Design Optimization

Optimization frameworks for expensive engineering simulations, including multi-objective and many-fidelity settings.

Scientific Machine Learning

Integration of machine learning with physical models for design exploration, prediction, and interpretability.

Marine and Aerospace Applications

Applications to ships, propellers, underwater vehicles, airfoils, and advanced transportation systems.

docs.title: Join the Group
docs.text:
We welcome interest from students, visiting researchers, postdoctoral researchers, and collaborators working on:

  • optimization
  • machine learning for engineering
  • scientific computing
  • marine and aerospace design
  • reduced-order modeling

docs.title: Potential Topics
docs.text:
Examples of thesis or research topics include:

  • dimensionality reduction for engineering design
  • multi-fidelity surrogate modeling
  • explainable optimization workflows
  • bio-inspired underwater vehicle design
  • physics-informed machine learning

docs.title: How to Apply
docs.text:
Please send:

  • a short motivation message
  • CV
  • research interests
  • optional transcript or portfolio of relevant work

to the contact listed on the Contact page.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Our research focuses on computational methods for engineering design under complexity, uncertainty, and high computational cost.

We are particularly interested in methods that improve:

  • design-space exploration
  • optimization efficiency
  • physical interpretability
  • reproducibility of computational results

docs.title: Design-space dimensionality reduction
docs.text:
We develop methods to reduce the dimensionality of high-dimensional parametric design spaces while preserving relevant geometric and physical information.

Representative topics include:

  • principal component analysis and modal parameterization
  • parametric model embedding (PME)
  • physics-informed and physics-driven dimensionality reduction
  • backmapping and parametric consistency

docs.title: Simulation-based design optimization
docs.text:
We investigate optimization strategies for expensive numerical simulations, including:

  • single- and multi-objective optimization
  • surrogate-based optimization
  • adaptive sampling
  • many-fidelity and multi-fidelity strategies
  • robust and uncertainty-aware optimization

docs.title: Scientific machine learning
docs.text:
We study how machine learning can support engineering design through:

  • surrogate modeling
  • reduced-order modeling
  • data-driven representation learning
  • explainability for design decisions
  • hybrid physics-data-driven approaches

docs.title: Application domains
docs.text:
Typical application domains include:

  • ship hull design
  • marine propellers
  • autonomous underwater vehicles
  • bio-inspired marine systems
  • airfoils and aerodynamic configurations
  • multidisciplinary vehicle design

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text:
Our methodological approach emphasizes:

  • physical consistency
  • computational efficiency
  • interpretability
  • reproducibility
  • open and reusable research software

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This section collects curated datasets developed within the MAO research group for:

  • design-space dimensionality reduction
  • surrogate-based optimization
  • time-series modeling and system identification

All datasets are publicly available on :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1} and are part of the MAO research group collection:

👉 https://zenodo.org/communities/cnr-inm-mao/

The collection ensures consistency, traceability, and long-term accessibility of all published datasets.

docs.title: 🔷 Dimensionality Reduction Benchmarks
docs.text:
Datasets designed for design-space compression, PME-based methods, and optimization workflows.

These datasets share a common structure:

  • geometry (D)
  • design variables (U)
  • optional physics fields
  • performance outputs

Available datasets

  • Bio-inspired underwater glider
  • RAE2822 airfoil
  • DTMB 5415 hull

👉 Explore dimensionality reduction datasets

docs.title: 🌊 Time-Series & Dynamics Datasets
docs.text:
Datasets focused on temporal evolution of physical systems, including ship motions and wave-induced responses.

These datasets are suitable for:

  • system identification
  • reduced-order modeling
  • machine learning for time-dependent phenomena

Available datasets

  • Ship motion time-series dataset

👉 Explore time-series datasets

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text:
All datasets include:

  • full metadata
  • variable definitions
  • preprocessing details
  • standardized formats

They are designed for:

  • fair benchmarking
  • cross-method comparison
  • integration with PME-toolkit workflows

docs.title: Citation
docs.text: Please cite the corresponding Zenodo records when using these datasets.

docs.title: Dimensionality Reduction Datasets
docs.text: This collection includes benchmark datasets for design-space dimensionality reduction and optimization.

docs.title: 🐠 Bio-Inspired Underwater Glider
docs.text: 👉 Full dataset page DOI: https://zenodo.org/records/18936594

docs.title: ✈️ RAE2822 Airfoil
docs.text: 👉 Full dataset page DOI: https://zenodo.org/records/18958555

docs.title: 🚢 DTMB 5415 Hull
docs.text: 👉 Full dataset page DOI: https://zenodo.org/records/19222008

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Benchmark dataset for the evaluation and comparison of design-space dimensionality reduction methods in hydrodynamic shape optimization.

The dataset is based on the DTMB 5415 hull and provides a physics-informed representation combining geometry, design variables, and hydrodynamic quantities.

DOI: https://zenodo.org/records/19222008

docs.title: Dataset Description
docs.text:
• Total configurations: 9,001
  • Baseline configuration: 1 (DTMB 5415 reference hull)
  • Sampled configurations: 9,000
  • Invalid simulations: 0

Configurations are generated through Monte Carlo sampling of a nonlinear parametric model based on global 3D shape modifications.

Each configuration is described by 27 normalized design variables in the range [-1, 1].

docs.title: Data Structure
docs.text: Each configuration includes:

docs.title: Geometry (D)
docs.text:
• Hull surface discretized with 2,250 points
  • Flattened coordinate vectors:
  • x, y, z

docs.title: Design Variables (U)
docs.text:
• 27 parameters defining geometric perturbations
  • Normalized in [-1, 1]

docs.title: Physics Fields
docs.text:
• Pressure distribution on hull (2,250 points)
  • Wave elevation field (1,500-point structured grid)

docs.title: Integrated Quantities
docs.text:
• Wave resistance coefficient (Cw)
  • RMS vertical acceleration at the bridge (az)
  • RMS pitch motion (ξ₅)

docs.title: Simulation Setup
docs.text:
Hydrodynamic quantities are computed using low-fidelity solvers:

  • WARP (linear potential flow) → calm-water resistance
  • SMP (linear strip theory) → seakeeping

Conditions

  • Head waves
  • Sea state 5
  • Bretschneider spectrum
  • Froude numbers: 0.25, 0.33, 0.41

docs.title: Scale Consistency
docs.text:
The dataset combines model-scale and full-scale representations:

  • Geometry provided at full scale (Lpp = 142 m)
  • Seakeeping quantities evaluated at full scale
  • Calm-water quantities computed at model scale (Lpp = 5.720 m)

docs.title: Data Quality
docs.text:
• All configurations correspond to physically valid simulations
  • Invalid samples were removed during dataset generation
  • Final dataset contains only consistent hydrodynamic responses

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Dimensionality reduction (PME, PI-PME, PD-PME)
  • Multi-physics embedding
  • Surrogate-based optimization
  • Benchmarking DR methods in naval hydrodynamics

docs.title: Scientific Context
docs.text:
The dataset follows a physics-informed representation of the design space, where:

  • geometry
  • design parameters
  • physical responses

are consistently combined within a unified framework.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Benchmark dataset for dimensionality reduction and optimization of a bio-inspired autonomous underwater glider.

The dataset is designed to support PME-based methods, surrogate modeling, and multi-fidelity optimization workflows.

DOI: https://zenodo.org/records/18936594

docs.title: Dataset Description
docs.text:
• Number of configurations: 16,385
  • Design variables: 32
  • Sampling strategy: Sobol sequence
  • Geometry representation: multi-section parametric model
  • Discretization: 784 spatial elements

docs.title: Data Structure
docs.text:
The dataset follows a structured representation:

  • Geometry (D) 3 × 784 matrix (spatial coordinates)

  • Design variables (U) 32-dimensional parameter vector

  • Physics (optional) Pressure coefficient (Cp) distribution

  • Outputs

  • Drag
  • Lift

docs.title: Simulation Setup
docs.text:
• Flow model: potential flow solver with viscous correction
  • Freestream velocity: 0.25 m/s
  • Angle of attack: 8°
  • Fluid density: 1030 kg/m³

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Dimensionality reduction (PME, PI-PME, PD-PME)
  • Surrogate-based optimization
  • Multi-fidelity modeling
  • Explainable design-space compression

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
The dataset includes:

  • database.mat
  • range_design.mat
  • metadata files

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text: The dataset is fully documented and directly usable within PME-toolkit workflows.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Benchmark dataset for aerodynamic shape optimization and dimensionality reduction based on the RAE2822 airfoil.

DOI: https://zenodo.org/records/18958555

docs.title: Dataset Description
docs.text:
• Number of configurations: 16,385
  • Design variables: 20
  • Parameterization: basis function expansion
  • Discretization: 129 surface points

docs.title: Data Structure
docs.text:
• Geometry (D) Airfoil coordinates (x, y)

  • Design variables (U) 20 parameters in normalized range [0,1]

  • Physics

  • Pressure coefficient (Cp)

  • Outputs

  • Lift coefficient (Cl)
  • Drag coefficient (Cd)
  • Moment coefficient (Cm)

docs.title: Simulation Setup
docs.text:
• Solver: XFOIL
  • Mach number: 0.4
  • Reynolds number: 6.5 × 10⁶
  • Angle of attack: 0°

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• Benchmarking dimensionality reduction methods
  • Surrogate model validation
  • Aerodynamic optimization

docs.title: Files
docs.text:
• database.mat
  • metadata and documentation files

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text: All configurations are pre-validated (no NaNs) and suitable for benchmarking studies.

docs.title: Time-Series Datasets
docs.text: This collection includes datasets for time-dependent physical systems.

docs.title: 🌊 Ship Motions Dataset
docs.text: 👉 Full dataset page DOI: https://zenodo.org/records/17315035

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Experimental dataset collected during a campaign conducted at the CNR-INM "Emilio Castagneto" towing tank (Rome) on the Codevintec CK14-e autonomous surface vehicle (ASV).

The dataset includes raw and processed time-series data, as well as synchronized experimental videos.

DOI: https://zenodo.org/records/17315035

docs.title: Experimental Setup
docs.text:
Experiments were conducted in the CNR-INM towing tank using a moored configuration of the CK14-e ASV.

Test conditions include:

  • Irregular waves

  • Pierson–Moskowitz spectrum

  • Regular waves

  • wavelength ratios: λ/L = 1, 1.5, 2, 3

docs.title: Dataset Content
docs.text: The dataset is organized into three main components:

docs.title: 🎥 Raw Video Data
docs.text:
File: ck14gopro_videos.zip

Contains GoPro Hero 7 recordings of the experiments:

  • c15.cam0.avi, GH010194.mp4, GH010195.mp4 → moored tests in irregular waves (Pierson–Moskowitz)

  • GH010196.mp4 → moored tests in regular waves (λ/L = 1, 1.5, 2, 3)

docs.title: 📡 Raw Sensor Data
docs.text:
File: ck14rawdata.zip

Includes measurements from onboard sensors and wave probes:

  • 250307_IRR_ss7_PM_moored_* → irregular wave tests (Pierson–Moskowitz)

  • 250307_REG_WP_PM_moored_1 → regular wave tests (λ/L = 1, 1.5, 2, 3)

  • 250307_ZERO_* → calibration data for wave probes

docs.title: 📊 Post-Processed Data
docs.text:
File: ck14postprocessed.zip

Processed datasets ready for analysis:

  • ck14pp_c15_1, ck14pp_c16_1, ck14pp_c17_1 → irregular wave tests

  • ck14pp_c18_1, ck14pp_c18_2, ck14pp_c18_3, ck14pp_c18_4 → regular wave tests (λ/L = 1, 1.5, 2, 3)

docs.title: Data Characteristics
docs.text:
• Time-series signals from onboard instrumentation
  • Wave elevation measurements
  • Synchronized experimental video recordings
  • Calibration datasets for probe correction

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
• System identification
  • Time-series modeling
  • Validation of numerical solvers
  • Machine learning for dynamical systems
  • Experimental benchmarking

docs.title: Reproducibility
docs.text:
The dataset includes:

  • raw measurements
  • processed signals
  • calibration data

enabling reproducible analysis workflows and validation studies.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This section collects research projects carried out within or in connection with the MAO Research Group.

Each project is documented through a dedicated internal page providing a concise overview of objectives, scientific scope, and related resources. When available, project-specific external websites are linked from the corresponding page.

docs.title: Past Projects
docs.text: BIODRONES 2023–2025 Biomimetic and Innovative Optimized hydrodynamic concepts of high-efficiency underwater gliding DRONES for ocean research

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
BIODRONES is a research project focused on biomimetic and innovative hydrodynamic concepts for high-efficiency underwater gliding drones for ocean research.

Within the MAO Research Group activities, the project provides an application context for research on:

  • simulation-based design optimization
  • dimensionality reduction
  • surrogate-based modeling
  • machine learning for engineering design

This page provides a concise overview within the MAO Research Group website. Broader project-specific material is available through dedicated external resources.

docs.title: Scientific Scope
docs.text:
The objective of BIODRONES is to provide the scientific and industrial communities with a systematic hydrodynamic optimization study on biomimetic and innovative hydrodynamic configurations for autonomous underwater gliders intended for ocean surveying.

The project targets compact configurations, conceived to fit in a station-wagon trunk or van, while aiming at high hydrodynamic efficiency, range, and endurance under given design specifications and constraints.

The initiative is also intended to support affordable ocean data collection, ocean digitalization, and possible technology transfer toward SMEs and industries active in underwater robotics.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The MAO Research Group develops research software to support:

  • simulation-based design optimization
  • dimensionality reduction
  • surrogate modeling
  • reproducible computational workflows

All software is designed with a strong focus on scientific rigor, transparency, and reproducibility.

docs.title: PME-toolkit
docs.text:
Toolkit for parametric model embedding and related methods for design-space dimensionality reduction.

👉 Open PME-toolkit page

docs.title: Software Principles
docs.text:
Our software development follows these principles:

  • scientific transparency
  • reproducibility
  • versioned releases
  • maintainable documentation
  • clear separation between stable and experimental features

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
PME-toolkit is an open-source framework for design-space dimensionality reduction based on the Parametric Model Embedding (PME) methodology and its physics-aware extensions.

The toolkit supports reproducible workflows for engineering design optimization and reduced-order modeling.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• reduced-order representation of design spaces
  • analytical backmapping to original variables
  • support for physics-informed (PI-PME) and physics-driven (PD-PME) variants
  • unified MATLAB and Python implementations
  • standardized input/output configuration (JSON-based workflows)

Example of first three PI-PME modes (glider dataset)

Mode 1

Mode 2

Mode 3

docs.title: Capabilities
docs.text:
PME-toolkit enables:

  • dimensionality reduction of high-dimensional design spaces
  • integration of geometry, design variables, and physics
  • explainable surrogate-based optimization
  • reproducible benchmarking workflows

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• 🔗 GitHub Repository
  • 📖 Documentation
  • 📦 PyPI
  • 📄 Zenodo
  • 📝 JOSS submission: under review

docs.title: Related Datasets
docs.text:
PME-toolkit is designed to work with benchmark datasets available in this repository:

  • Bio-inspired underwater glider
  • RAE2822 airfoil

👉 See Datasets

docs.title: Citation
docs.text: If you use PME-toolkit in your research, please cite the associated publication (JOSS or related articles).

docs.title: Development Status
docs.text:
• actively developed
  • tested on benchmark datasets
  • aligned MATLAB/Python implementations
